topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment was deafening, a heavy blanket smothering any lingering cheer. I stared at the crumpled invitation on the coffee table, the cheerful script mocking me now. "Party cancelled." The words echoed in my ears, hollow and sharp. A cold knot tightened in my chest, spreading outward until my breath caught in my throat. I replayed the last few weeks in my mind—the frantic texts, the carefully planned decorations, the excited chatter I’d shared with everyone. All of it felt suddenly tainted, a clumsy, selfish display of anticipation. I picked up my phone, my fingers trembling slightly, and scrolled through the group chat, the unread messages burning behind my eyes. I needed to call Sarah, but the words wouldn't form. I just sat there, the weight of my oversight pressing down, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

When Mark finally delivered the news, the air in the room seemed to thin. He said it in a low, measured tone, and the casual lightness that had permeated the evening evaporated instantly. I felt a distinct physical clenching in my jaw. My gaze dropped to my hands, noticing the slight tremor as I gripped my glass. Every detail of the evening—the chosen music, the specific shade of balloons—suddenly seemed absurd, a monument to my own misplaced focus. I wanted to apologize, to rewind time, but the words caught in my throat, tasting like ash. I started pacing the length of the living room, the sound of my own footsteps too loud in the sudden quiet. I kept running the scenario over and over: the disappointment on their faces, the letdown. A deep, uncomfortable ache settled behind my ribs, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

I found myself standing by the window, watching the streetlights blur through the rain-streaked glass. The cancellation notice lay beside me, stark white against the dark wood of the windowsill. A profound sense of failure washed over me, a heavy, sinking feeling in my stomach. It wasn't just about the event; it was about the effort I’d expended, the expectation I’d built up around this moment. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to push the image of their disappointment away, but it clung to me like damp fabric. I walked over to the stereo, my movements slow and deliberate, and turned the volume down, letting the quiet amplify the hollowness inside. I felt a desperate urge to fix things, to somehow undo the mistake, but all I could manage was a slow, defeated sigh, the sound barely audible above the drumming rain.
